title: Late-Time Relaxation from Landau Singularities
abstract: Nonlinear hydrodynamic interactions can change the relaxation of fluctuations from exponential to power-law decay at late times. Schwinger-Keldysh effective field theory provides a standard framework for describing such fluctuation effects, where the nonlinear late-time behavior is encoded in loop corrections. Extracting this behavior requires identifying the singularities of loop integrals, whose structure becomes increasingly intricate beyond simple models. We apply Landau singularity analysis to two-point functions in effective field theories and determine the singularities induced by nonlinear interactions without performing the loop integrations explicitly. From these frequency-space singularities, we extract nonlinear relaxation modes that control the late-time behavior. When gapless modes are present, these modes produce power-law decay at late times. Our results give a systematic singularity-based description of nonlinear late-time relaxation in a broad class of macroscopic effective theories.
summary: The paper presents a systematic method to identify how nonlinear interactions in effective field theories lead to power-law relaxation at late times. By using Landau singularity analysis, the authors can determine the late-time decay behavior without performing explicit loop integrations. This provides a unified description of long-time tails in a wide range of macroscopic systems.
main_problem: Understanding how nonlinear hydrodynamic interactions cause the relaxation of fluctuations to transition from exponential decay to power-law decay (long-time tails) at late times.
main_result: The authors establish a systematic way to identify nonlinear decay modes and power-law exponents using Landau singularity analysis, showing that nonlinear interactions generate new decay modes and universal power-law behavior in the presence of gapless modes.
method: Landau singularity analysis applied to Schwinger-Keldysh effective field theory, using Landau equations to identify loop integral singularities and inverse Fourier transforms to extract time-domain asymptotic behavior.
model_or_system: A broad class of macroscopic effective theories described by the Schwinger-Keldysh effective field theory (SK-EFT) framework, specifically focusing on near-equilibrium systems with gapless hydrodynamic modes and nonlinear mode coupling.
key_observables: Two-point correlation functions (retarded, advanced, and symmetrized correlators) and the time-domain signal decay.
important_parameters: Late-time regime (t -> infinity), long-wavelength limit (k -> 0), spatial dimension (d), and the minimum loop number (L_min) determined by the interaction power.
main_assumptions: The free two-point functions possess simple poles in the complex frequency plane, and the functions vanish sufficiently fast at infinity to ensure the convergence of the inverse Fourier transform.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides an illustration of a 'banana diagram' (a 1PI topology) used to represent the loop integrals and the structure of the self-energy analysis.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of nonlinear relaxation, defines the SK-EFT framework, applies Landau singularity analysis to identify loop-induced singularities, derives the resulting power-law decay in the time domain, and provides a unified description for a broad class of theories.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for many-body dynamics as it provides a universal, systematic framework for predicting long-time tails and non-exponential relaxation in any system governed by nonlinear hydrodynamic-like effective field theories.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Illustration of a banana diagram. Wavy and solid lines represent the φa and φr legs, respectively. The external momentum is k; pl denote the independent loop momenta, and qe the internal momenta carried by the propagators. For illustration, we choose a simple routing in which qe = pe for e < E and qE = k −PL l=1 pl.
